speaker: interviewer
text: --life. Mrs. Sklaver, you had just moved to New Jersey, and you were raising your family here. And tell me how your life has played out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So far, as you can see, it all was on the way up, always improving, always better. And of course, it's a great accomplishment to-- to come to America and, 10 years later, to buy a house. In 1959, we bought the house. And my husband was the only provider. The children were in school, and I was home. I did a lot of cleaning. And they were very annoyed with that. And they used to say, stop cleaning. Go out and get a life. And I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I did not have the-- the-- the diploma, the high school diploma with me. So I had to finish here and get a high school equivalency diploma. In Fair Lawn High School, I did that. And then I volunteered at the Daughters of Miriam for, you know, just, you know, to be out of the house. And it was sad, but I knew that I have a calling. And I-- I figured I'd better go to school and-- and study. And I enrolled in the-- during the summer, in the Bergen Community College and took some courses, so it wouldn't be as hard. You know, English and-- and math, it was all new to me, new math and sciences. It should be easier for the year-- during the year. And that was in 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a degree did you get.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got a nursing degree. And I worked from 1974 until '81, at the Barnert Memorial Hospital in the-- in Paterson. It was very hard but rewarding. And I was considered one of the best nurses on the floor. It was a medical surgical floor, extremely difficult to-- to handle. And at first, I was full-time, and then I was only part-time and instead of-- but every other weekend still included. Yeah, and I worked there, because our children were already in colleges. And in-- and in-- and in '69, our older daughter, Linda, got married to Myron Schoenfeld. And-- and Sheila got married in '73.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sklaver, do you think that your Holocaust experience influenced the way you felt about your children and the way you raised them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so. I believe so. I feel that, from what I read and hear, they accomplished so much, the-- the survivors and the second generation of the survivors. I wanted to tell the children about my-- my horrible times. And they were too young. But when they were older, I couldn't talk about it anymore. And those were the worst times of my life. Because I-- at first, I wanted to-- to-- to share with them. I couldn't even share with the family that was here in-- in America, because they-- they didn't understand. American people were concerned that they didn't have enough. They had to eat chicken instead of meat. I mean there was ridiculous certain remarks. And I met a lot of survivors. And they're life was not as easy as mine, here, in America. And I-- I hoped, you know, that it will never happen any place or definitely not in America. I was very protective of the children, very protective. I worried all the time, worried all the time. If-- if--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you worry about?

speaker: interviewee
text: --if they were late from school, I would be worried. They could not join any after-school activity, because they have to be home on time. And-- and apparently, it carried over some sort of a-- of a fear. But in time, it-- it-- it wore off. It wore off. Yes. I wasn't cleaning, anymore, the house. I wasn't worrying about cleaning the house. I was doing useful work. And I was earning, of course, the first time that I-- I also a bread-winner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to say, any message you would have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, that whether I believe that I survived, I believed I will survive. But no one knew what it means, the word 'survive.' Because in our parts, there were no camps. There were no gas chambers. All it was is just graves and mass killings. And--and we were not only treated by the Germans, so badly, but also by the Ukrainians. And so when I thought of surviving, I always thought, I will land in Palestine, where I can be free. This I even dreamt when I was in-- in-- in the forest, at Marina's. And that helped me, too, to be there, to spend the days, the endless days. I had not spoken Yiddish or Hebrew until I met this person in Rowne. I was afraid that I will, you know, give myself away. So I tried to dream. And I had a tendency of speaking in my sleep. So I would not speak any Yiddish. I did not imagine that what happened, all to the better, that I would survive and some day-- [SOBBING] some day I will be married and have children. Because from what we heard that no one-- there were not too many survivors. And I was told, by an astrologer, [? Stella ?] [NON-ENGLISH], you will travel a very long distance. You will be in America. And she didn't say, America. You will travel a long distance. And you will come to a country. And you will have furs and jewelry. [SOBBING] I [? do ?] have them. [CRYING] [SOBBING] But the most prized jewels is my husband and my daughter, Linda, son-in-law Myron, and Sheila, our daughter, Sheila, son-in-law Myron-- son-in-law Larry, and my granddaughter, Zoe. This is Linda's daughter. And granddaughter Abby and the twin grandsons, David and Jonathan, Sheila and Larry's children. [SOBBING] And he has not succeeded. The Jews had many, many [? Herrmanns ?] and Hitlers. I think he probably was the worst. So from what I hear, how other-- other survivors accomplishing what they accomplished and the second generation, [SOBBING] what they did. I don't know what the lesson there to the Jewish people and to world, at large.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] [SOBBING] We certainly can say, am Yisrael chai ya chai ya. [CRYING] Thank you for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] You're welcome. May I also add that, if anyone of the interviewers are as capable and as compassionate as Ruth Meyer, I think the Shoah Foundation is in good hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank you, Richard, for being patient and so well doing your job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, Mordechai Kritczmar, he was born in the town of Hoszcza. He-- they married approximately 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken, I think, before he got married, before he got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the town of Hoszcza.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hoszcza, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Hinda Kritczmar. Her maiden name was [NON-ENGLISH]. She was born in Berdychiv and married in the town of Hoszcza. It was the times of the czarist Russia, still.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken just before she got married. And-- and she perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: The pictures, they were at my cousin's in-- in New-- in New York. And they gave me the pictures, and I enlarged them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They must have been photo-- postcard-sized pictures. And I enlarged them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my sister, Leah, Leah Kritczmar Finkelstein. She was briefly married. And then-- and she perished in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: When and where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in-- during the Polish independent, like when Poland was an independent state. And it was a school picture for her-- for a special course that she was studying in Vilna for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me. I am-- I'm 9-- I'm 12 years old. And I'm visiting our family in Rowne. I was always with my mother going to visit the family in Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in--

speaker: interviewer
text: 1937, does that sound right?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1937? It was just before the war. Yeah. On the left is my sister, Paula, the one that survived in Russia. And I am on-- on the right. This is also a visit to Rowne in the backyard of our family. It was a visit from a cousin that came to visit from America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Picture was taken of this school, or our school [NON-ENGLISH] is [NON-ENGLISH]. And-- When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bangs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the third row, on the left-hand side, with the bangs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And your name there? Yaffa, Yaffa Kritczmar.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, [NON-ENGLISH], on his way to the-- to Palestine, as a halutz, with his wife, [? Rajzel ?] [NON-ENGLISH] and Kritczmar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The blond one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, is he the middle row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle row.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife is next to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his wife is in the beret, in the blond beret, in the white beret.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of our town Hoszcza, with the fire department, with the gate to the park, where military always was stationed. A few homes, you can see. Our house is not seen here. It was far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939 was the picture taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the Polish man, [POLISH] Kaspierkawicz, that I stayed with and worked for the family. But even though he knew I was Jewish, at that end, he never harmed me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken-- I was already in America, and I received that from them in a letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the boss's wife, my [NON-ENGLISH]. Her name was [POLISH], [POLISH] Kaspierkawicz. Both of them are not alive anymore. And the picture was sent to me, from Poland, to us, in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Bytom. We were getting married. And we got married in 1946, February 2nd. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me who is in this picture? Give the names, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband of 50 years. We celebrated our 50th anniversary last February, Yitzchak Sklaver, Irving Sklaver in America, and Sonia Sklaver.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of-- a wedding picture, of me of my husband, taken in 1946, February 3rd.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bytom, Niederschlesien, the new Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yitzchak Sklaver, Irving Sklaver in America. And I am Sonia Sklaver in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Mrs. Sklaver, we'd like you to introduce us to your lovely family.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Irving Sklaver, our older daughter, Linda, Linda Schoenfeld, her husband, Myron Schoenfeld--

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Zoe, our granddaughter, Zoe, and our daughter, Sheila, her husband, Larry, Larry Kantor, Abby, our granddaughter, Abby, their daughter, and their twin grandsons, our twin grandsons, David and Jonathan.

speaker: interviewee
text: David and Jonathan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is there anything that either one of you would like to say, at this time, about your experience and how it all connects to your family and what you've achieved here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to start from the beginning, if I may. For my little girl, which I knew her in Hoszcza. We went together to school. And I used to look at her [INAUDIBLE], [? her ?] [? hair, ?] and I always was thinking, this is the kind of girl I would really love to have for my wife. And nobody knew at that time. And I was thinking ahead, way ahead. And then started the war. The war started. I went out, run away. I went all the way to Asia. Everybody knows Tashkent and that. When the war finished, I sent a letter to Hoszcza. We had a neighbor, the priest. And I knew he's a gentile. And I think he would know what happened to our family. He knew the family very well, everybody. I don't know if he'll remember me. I was the youngest one in the family. But anyway, I sent him a letter, find out-- to find out what happened to our family. In no time, I got an answer from him. I'm sorry to tell you, all your family disappeared, were killed. It was very hard to take. I was looking in the papers, all the time, if I will see when the Russian Army moved closer to our town. It never were mentioned. And I was thinking, my parents, they had friends, and they [? were ?] [? still ?] [? alive. ?] And I received a letter, nobody is alive. And I asked him what to do. And he's telling me, I don't think you have anything to do to come home. No-- no survivors from you family. And he gave me some names who survived. And between them was a little girl with a name of Szunia, from name, this is what he used to call her. My heart was beating, the youngest one from her whole family. I knew her very well. Their store and our store was close, each to the other. And we saw in the school all the time. And this little girl survived. My, brothers such a strong people, who were hiding, could have-- were hiding in any place, in [? Manevychi ?] and so. And they were killed. And this little girl is a miracle. And I was the youngest one in my family. And I survived. Everything was going on so fast. And I start to plan how to go on. [CRYING] [SOBBING] I came to Hoszcza. I didn't see nobody. I was walking like to the cemetery to visit my mother's grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: A schoolteacher met me at that time. She knew Sonia. She knew me. And she said, Mr. Sklaver, what are you doing here? How did you survive and so on? You better get out from here. You will be killed. We will kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SOBBING] I turn around. I didn't even go to the cemetery. I ran back to the town, the next one, Hoszcza, [POLISH]. From Hoszcza to [POLISH] was like 30 kilometer. My brother was there, already, with his wife. They left Russia before me. And they didn't stay too long. I don't remember exactly, but a week or two weeks. They were heading to Poland. We can look in Poland. We have nobody to look, but I have name for Szunia. We went to Warsaw. Over there was the joint distribution, and they had a Jewish committee. And they kept records from all of the Jewish people coming from Ukraine and from Russia, from all over Poland. I looked. But I didn't find her name. And this is the only I'm that I'm looking. I went to Lodz. And in Lodz, I was very lucky. We saw the name from her, the [? Gellman ?] family were registered. And they were heading-- left for Beuthen-- Bytom, Niederschlesien. I talked to my brother, that named [? Avi, ?] we're going to Beuthen. We're looking for Szunia, so huh. We went to Beuthen. We went to the committee, again, and found her name and address. And I found her. I found her with family [? Gellman, ?] because [? Gellman ?] was a family. And they supported her. And she stayed with them and this. And I started to date her, [? then, ?] [? we two. ?] We started to go out in the morning till the evening. I slept over there, next-door. In the morning, she was going to the park, helping out the family to make a certain living, selling cigarette and some other things, you know. And I kept my eyes on her, already, you know. And at that time, starting, if you remember, the Kielce progroms, again, killing Jews. And I said to her, Szunia, we have to leave from here. We have to go away. It will happen again. We have nothing to do here. We go to Israel. Let's go to Germany. From Germany, go to Israel and so on. Well, she was very smart. And she said to me, I'm not going with an unmarried man. You're a young man, and I'm young. And you aren't going with the family. And I wouldn't leave. Then I said, all right, let's get married. She didn't think too long, but she didn't give me a yes for an answer, right away. It was very hard to take it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the reason I asked her? Because I knew her from before. If my father would be alive, and her father and family, and if you know that should I marry Szunia, she would be accepted-- [CRYING] [SOBBING] --as a daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any girls in the family, only boys. And we would be proud to have a girl like that with us-- [CRYING] and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I tell you? I was very happy we married. She asked her brother an OK, till we got the letter. We got married. And I think I'm happy. She's a happy. And we have a wonderful family, wonderful two children, grandchildren, wonderful two son-in-laws, Myron and Larry. We love them all. [SOBBING] And what can I tell you? I love you guys. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we going to go around to the family? We still got a couple minutes on this tape?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is there anybody else who'd like to say something? I'm interested, from the daughters, in how much you heard about your parent's war experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: It might be safe to go to another tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's-- let's stop here and put in a new tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll take one of those tissues.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I need a better tissue.
